channel.title: Debt Desk
channel.description: Daily briefing with Morning Brief-style national and Columbus news plus commercial real estate debt and multifamily market coverage. Deals, SOFR and Treasury curve context, CMBS, debt funds, Fannie Mae, Freddie Mac, and HUD execution. Produced by Jeff Bechtel.
channel.itunes:author: Jeff Bechtel

item.title: Debt Desk — Debt Desk for April 3: National and Columbus Updates, Then CRE Debt and Multifamily Execution
item.description: Today’s Debt Desk opens with fresh national and Columbus headlines, then moves into the debt desk read on the Treasury curve, SOFR publication timing, CMBS stress, lender execution across banks, life companies, CMBS and debt funds, plus multifamily agency, HUD, and securitized market signals.
item.content:encoded:
Good morning. It is Friday, April 3rd, 2026, and this is Debt Desk.

Let’s get into it.

In national news, Washington is still trying to manage a war story, a trade story, and a funding story at the same time, and markets are watching all three.

The biggest geopolitical headline is the White House trying to steady public nerves over Iran. In a major address on April 2nd, President Trump asked Americans for more time and said the operation was progressing, but he still did not give a clear end point. That matters because the market question is no longer just whether the United States can sustain military pressure. The question is whether the administration can define an endgame clearly enough to calm oil, shipping, and risk sentiment. So far, that answer is still incomplete.

Trade policy is also back in the spotlight in a very direct way. On April 2nd, the administration announced a new executive order that could impose tariffs of up to one hundred percent on some patented pharmaceutical imports if companies do not strike pricing deals and move production onshore. That is a major escalation in the administration’s effort to tie trade, industrial policy, and healthcare pricing together. For businesses and investors, the immediate takeaway is that tariff risk is no longer limited to the usual manufacturing and metals categories. It is broadening again.

At the same time, the partial Homeland Security shutdown remains a real operational and political problem even though leaders are trying to contain the damage. Republican leaders said on April 1st that they have a path to fund most of the department, and President Trump separately said he would move to restore pay for unpaid Homeland Security workers. But the fact that this is still unresolved after weeks of disruption tells you something important about the current Washington backdrop: even when leadership converges on a plan, execution is not automatic.

And hanging over this Friday morning is the labor market. The March jobs report is due later today, April 3rd, and economists have been looking for a rebound after February’s ugly reading. The market is watching that release for two reasons. First, it will shape expectations for consumer resilience going into the second quarter. Second, it will tell us whether higher energy costs and war-related uncertainty are starting to bleed into hiring. If payroll growth looks soft again, recession talk will get louder very quickly. If it looks better, the conversation shifts back toward inflation, oil, and how long rates stay higher.

So that is the national setup this morning: war uncertainty, broader tariff pressure, unfinished shutdown politics, and a labor-market report that could reset expectations for growth before the weekend is over.

Now let’s come back home to Columbus and Central Ohio.

The most immediate pocketbook story is power. WOSU reported on April 2nd that the Public Utilities Commission of Ohio approved changes to AEP Ohio’s rate structure. The commission allowed an increase in base distribution revenues, though far below what the utility originally requested, and it also approved a minimum monthly charge structure for new data-center customers. That matters locally because Central Ohio keeps attracting major power-hungry development, and the public fight is increasingly about who pays for the grid buildout that growth requires.

That question got even sharper in a separate April 2nd report from WOSU on a proposed one point one billion dollar set of transmission lines tied in part to data-center demand. The Ohio Consumers’ Counsel is asking federal regulators to reject or delay the project, arguing that households could end up carrying too much of the cost. This is becoming one of the defining Central Ohio stories of 2026. Growth is real, the need for infrastructure is real, but so is the political risk if residents believe they are subsidizing hyperscale expansion without clear consumer protections.

There is also a Columbus story with national reach, even if it comes through sports. Axios Columbus reported on April 2nd that the Save The Crew movement helped inspire the newly introduced Home Team Act in Congress. The bill would require owners of pro sports teams to give local communities a chance to buy a franchise before relocation. Whether or not that measure advances, it is another example of Columbus exporting a policy model rather than just reacting to one. For a city trying to define itself as more than a fast-growing Midwest market, that kind of institutional influence matters.

Taken together, Columbus this morning looks like a city wrestling with second-order growth issues. The market is not asking whether the region is expanding. It is asking how energy costs, infrastructure demands, and civic leverage get managed as that expansion accelerates.

Now let’s move into the Debt Desk core.

The first thing to know this morning is that the rate backdrop is still workable, but it is not easy.

According to the U.S. Treasury’s April 2nd curve, the two-year closed around three point seven nine, the five-year around three point nine four, the ten-year around four point three one, and the thirty-year around four point eight eight. That is not just a ten-year story. The whole curve matters right now.

The front end tells you floating-rate borrowers are still dealing with a meaningful cost of carry. The five-year and ten-year matter for a huge share of permanent-loan pricing conversations. And the thirty-year reminds you that long-duration money still carries a real premium. That shape continues to push borrowers toward shorter terms, more optionality, and more careful refinance planning.

On SOFR, today has an unusual wrinkle. The New York Fed said in advance that there would be no SOFR publication on Friday, April 3rd, because repo market conventions around Good Friday and settlement timing disrupt the usual calculation process. So the practical message for the debt markets today is continuity, not surprise. SOFR is still sitting in the mid-three point six range, but today’s absence of a fresh print is a calendar issue, not a stress signal.

Now to the real credit read.

Deals are getting done, but only if the structure respects the market we are actually in. The latest Mortgage Bankers Association data showed commercial and multifamily mortgage borrowing ran thirty percent above the prior year in the fourth quarter of 2025, with depositories up sharply and investor-driven lenders, CMBS lenders, life companies, and the GSEs all higher as well. MBA also said this week that the top mortgage originators in 2025 included JLL, JPMorgan, Eastdil, Newmark, Wells Fargo, CBRE, Walker and Dunlop, KeyBank, Berkadia, and Bank of America. That tells you capital is not absent. It tells you capital is competitive where the deal fits.

Banks are participating, but usually with more discipline on proceeds, cash management, and sponsorship scrutiny than borrowers got used to in the easy-money cycle. Life companies remain selective and are still most attractive on high-quality, lower-leverage permanent executions where certainty matters more than maximum leverage. CMBS is open, but borrowers need to respect credit screens, asset quality, and structure because securitized buyers are not giving away spread. Debt funds are still essential in the middle of the capital stack, especially for transitional business plans, lease-up, rescue capital, and bridge-to-agency strategies, but that flexibility comes at a higher coupon and with much tougher milestone expectations.

The CMBS picture captures that split better than almost anything else. Trepp’s March 31st delinquency report showed overall U.S. CMBS delinquency rising to seven point five five percent. KBRA then said on April 1st that the delinquency rate in its private-label CMBS universe moved up to seven point seven percent in March, with the overall distress rate holding at ten point three percent. In other words, securitized credit is still functioning, but legacy stress is not gone. It is rotating.

And multifamily is no longer immune to that story. KBRA said multifamily distress increased sharply in March as more loans turned delinquent, including newer-vintage paper. That does not mean multifamily is broken. It means the market is finally separating strong rent rolls and realistic debt structures from deals that depended on perfect timing, tax assumptions, or cheap takeouts.

This is where lender tone matters more than headline volume.

For stabilized multifamily, agency remains one of the cleanest answers in the market. FHFA’s 2026 caps are eighty-eight billion dollars each for Fannie Mae and Freddie Mac, and both agencies entered the year with more room to support production. Fannie Mae said it provided roughly seventy-four billion dollars of multifamily financing in 2025, up thirty-four percent from the prior year. Freddie Mac said its 2025 production volume was seventy-seven point six billion dollars, up seventeen percent year over year, with structured products and forward commitments both contributing to liquidity. That matters because borrowers still need scalable, dependable permanent debt, and the agencies are signaling that they intend to be there.

The practical takeaway is this: if you have a stabilized or near-stabilized multifamily asset with a clean story, agency execution is still highly competitive. If you need speed, flexibility, rehab dollars, or a lighter hand around transition risk, the debt-fund lane may be the better first stop. And if you are in the gray zone between those worlds, the best transactions now are the ones that are explicitly underwritten as bridge-to-agency from day one.

CMBS also remains part of the multifamily conversation, but more as a discipline check than a universal answer. The market is open for stronger collateral and stronger sponsorship, yet the delinquency and distress data tell you investors are actively repricing operational misses. That means multifamily borrowers should not assume securitized execution will forgive weak coverage, aggressive exit assumptions, or unclear tax and reserve issues.

HUD and FHA remain important, especially for borrowers who value duration and mission alignment over sheer speed. Earlier this year HUD moved to level multifamily mortgage insurance premiums at twenty-five basis points across categories, which should help make FHA executions more straightforward on a cost basis. That does not change the fact that HUD deals still demand more patience and process management. But when a borrower wants durable long-term financing, affordability preservation, or a more insulated capital structure, HUD still deserves to be in the quote stack.

One more point on market tone before we wrap this section: shorter terms are still winning. MBA’s recent research has emphasized the same pattern people are seeing in live deal flow. Borrowers are putting a bigger premium on flexibility, extension options, and a future refinance path than on locking expensive duration at all costs. That is rational in a curve this steep. It is also why execution quality matters so much. If your loan is short, your takeout plan cannot be vague.

So what does all of this mean at the desk this morning?

It means refinance math is improving only selectively. It means bridge lenders still have a job to do. It means agencies remain central to multifamily liquidity. It means CMBS is active but unforgiving. And it means every borrower conversation should include not just today’s coupon, but the most likely refinance lane eighteen to thirty-six months from now.

Now a quick markets snapshot before we close.

Treasury yields as of the April 2nd close were roughly three point seven nine on the two-year, three point nine four on the five-year, four point three one on the ten-year, and four point eight eight on the thirty-year.

SOFR does not have a fresh April 3rd publication because of the New York Fed’s holiday-related schedule adjustment, so the operative read is that overnight funding remains in the mid-three point six range, with stability intact for now.

Trepp’s latest CMBS delinquency rate is seven point five five percent for March.

KBRA’s private-label CMBS delinquency rate is seven point seven percent, with distress at ten point three percent.

Agency capacity remains substantial with eighty-eight billion dollar caps for both Fannie Mae and Freddie Mac in 2026.

And Columbus continues to sit at the intersection of growth and infrastructure strain, especially on the power side.

One thing to watch today:

Watch the March jobs report and how the front end of the curve reacts. If payrolls come in firm and oil stays elevated, longer-for-longer rate expectations will harden again and that will keep refinance pressure alive. If the labor market softens materially, Treasury relief could help sentiment, but lenders will immediately shift their focus to recession risk and cash-flow durability.

The broader takeaway for this Friday is simple. There is capital in the market, but it is not charity capital. Borrowers can still get deals done across banks, life companies, CMBS, debt funds, Fannie, Freddie, and HUD. But the market wants realism now. It wants real reserves, real contingency planning, and real answers on the refinance path.

If you are heading into conversations today, do not sell perfection. Sell durability. Show how the asset performs if lease-up is slower, if expenses run hotter, or if the exit market stays selective longer than hoped. In this environment, the cleanest story is usually the most financeable story.

That is your Debt Desk for Friday, April 3rd, 2026.

I will see you Monday.

item.title: Debt Desk — Debt Desk Launch: National and Columbus Headlines, Then CRE Debt and Multifamily Execution
item.description: Today’s Debt Desk opens with Morning Brief-style national and Columbus headlines, then moves into the debt desk read on SOFR, Treasury curve moves, CMBS stress, debt-fund and CRE CLO momentum, and multifamily execution across agency and HUD channels.
item.content:encoded:
Good morning. It is Thursday, April 2nd, 2026, and this is Debt Desk.

Let’s get into it.

In national headlines, the White House war message and market nerves are still front and center.

In his first major national address since the conflict with Iran began, President Trump told Americans the operation was on track and urged patience, but he did not offer a clear end date. Associated Press reporting said the speech left many key strategy questions unanswered while markets continued to show stress around energy and geopolitical risk.

Elsewhere in the national macro picture, rates remain a major driver of business and consumer decision-making.

At the Treasury close on April 2nd, yields were roughly three point seven nine on the two-year, three point nine four on the five-year, four point three one on the ten-year, and four point eight eight on the thirty-year. The shape of that curve continues to frame expectations for financing costs, capital allocation, and consumer activity.

Overnight funding also remains relatively steady, though still elevated versus prior cycles.

The New York Fed’s latest SOFR print for April 1st came in at three point six five, with the thirty-day average right around the same level. That helps with confidence on floating-rate assumptions versus the whipsaw conditions we have seen in prior cycles. But we are still carrying coupons that are materially higher than the debt many sponsors are rolling off from 2021 and 2022. So even with stability, refinancing pain has not gone away.

At the same time, the broader national backdrop still shows a split between pockets of liquidity and pockets of stress.

We are seeing real issuance and transaction flow in parts of the credit stack, but also visible stress in legacy pockets. That split is likely to define the rest of 2026 more than any single rate print. Capital is available, but it is selective and pricing execution risk much more aggressively than it did in the low-rate era.

Now to Columbus and Central Ohio.

A notable state court development is moving through the local policy landscape.

WOSU reported on April 1st that the Ohio Supreme Court gave Columbus a procedural win in litigation over city gun restrictions, allowing the city to appeal a preliminary injunction path. The court did not rule on the core constitutionality question yet, but the decision keeps the legal fight active and relevant for city policy.

Housing supply and builder strategy also remain active local themes.

Builder Magazine reported April 2nd that Drees Homes is entering the Columbus market, calling it the company’s 12th operating market and third in Ohio. The article highlighted Columbus as an underbuilt housing market with strong job momentum and cited a twenty-two percent increase in tech jobs since 2023.

Quality-of-life and public investment remain another visible part of the regional story.

Axios Columbus, citing Bureau of Economic Analysis data, reported that outdoor recreation contributed about twenty billion dollars to Ohio’s 2024 economy, with more than one hundred fifty thousand jobs statewide, and noted that Columbus’ Great Southern Metro Park opened April 1st.

So that is your Columbus setup this morning: policy motion, housing expansion signals, and continued public investment in quality-of-life assets.

Now let’s shift into the Debt Desk core.

The first thing to understand right now is that deals are getting done, but the style of deal is different than what many teams got used to in 2021 and early 2022.

Today’s lenders are rewarding credible execution and punishing fragile business plans. The capital is there, but committees are requiring tighter assumptions, stronger sponsorship evidence, and better downside planning.

Three lanes are driving most of the action.

Lane one is refinance-and-extend with fresh equity support. This remains a major source of volume because so many maturities are still coming due at debt costs above existing notes.

Lane two is transitional bridge for multifamily and selected industrial where the business plan has clear, near-term cash flow milestones.

Lane three is securitized and structured channels for collateral that can clear increasingly disciplined buyer and rating expectations.

On the structured side, one of the most important positives this year has been the CRE CLO recovery in issuance pace. Trepp’s early-March read showed issuance around eleven point two billion dollars, materially above the same period last year. That is a meaningful sign that investor demand for transitional credit is functioning.

But let’s pair that with the other side of the tape.

Trepp’s March 31 update showed CMBS delinquency at seven point five five percent, up forty-one basis points month over month. So we have risk capital returning in some lanes while stress remains elevated in others. That is exactly the definition of a bifurcated market.

For borrowers, that means execution quality decides everything.

If you are bringing stable cash flow, defensible assumptions, and a documented operational plan, lenders are still willing to engage.

If you are bringing thin interest coverage, uncertain lease rollover, or a story that requires immediate cap-rate compression to work, expect lower leverage, wider spreads, and more structure.

Now to multifamily, because this is where many 2026 financing decisions are being made in real time.

At the agency level, FHFA’s 2026 caps remain eighty-eight billion dollars each for Fannie Mae and Freddie Mac, with mission requirements still central to program use. For qualified deals, agency execution continues to offer some of the strongest combinations of certainty, duration, and cost efficiency in the market.

Freddie’s small-balance channel also remains important because it keeps workforce and middle-market product connected to institutional capital pathways, not just the largest metro trophy assets.

The broader multifamily operating backdrop is still highly local.

In higher-supply metros, lenders are underwriting lease-up and concessions with much less optimism than they did two years ago. In lower-supply and more stable Midwest pockets, execution conditions are generally more constructive, especially for stabilized assets with clean trailing operations.

In other words, there is no single national multifamily underwriting story anymore. Submarket realities are dominating loan terms.

Debt funds continue to fill a critical role in this environment.

They are often the fastest option when sponsors need flexibility, speed, or structure outside bank and agency boxes. But that flexibility comes with higher cost and tighter expectations around milestones. The best outcomes we are seeing are when sponsors use debt funds as an intentional bridge to a defined takeout, not as an emergency patch.

HUD remains part of the conversation as well, especially for borrowers prioritizing long-duration certainty.

HUD execution is still slower than other channels, but in the right deal it can provide durable financing that is hard to replicate elsewhere. In a market where benchmark uncertainty remains elevated, that duration value is real.

Now let’s tie all this together into today’s practical playbook.

Quote across lanes, not just one lane. Borrowers make better decisions when they can see bridge, agency, and structured options side by side.

Underwrite to realistic occupancy and expense assumptions now, not optimistic stabilization narratives later.

Make your refinance plan explicit at closing. Do not wait until year two to decide the takeout path.

Keep a close eye on benchmark composition. The two-year, five-year, and thirty-year are all shaping decisions right now, not just the ten-year headline.

Treat volatility as a base case, not an exception case. Structure reserves and covenants accordingly.

Before we close, let me add a deeper debt-desk read on what is actually moving committees this week, because this is where deals are winning or dying.

On leverage, we continue to see lenders favoring a lower initial advance paired with future-funding flexibility, rather than stretching day-one proceeds. That approach gives sponsors capital for the plan, but only as execution proves out. In practice, that means the old conversation of “How much can I borrow today?” is being replaced by “How much can I earn as I hit milestones?” It is a healthier framework, but it requires better operating discipline.

On structure, reserves are now doing far more work than they did in the low-rate cycle. Interest reserves, TI and LC reserves, and capex escrows are not just check-the-box terms anymore. They are often the credit bridge that gets a deal from maybe to yes. If you are advising borrowers, be explicit about that early. A deal that looks expensive on coupon alone can still be the right execution if structure protects liquidity through the hardest part of the business plan.

On sponsorship, transparency is being priced in real time. Sponsors with clear monthly reporting, realistic assumptions, and an honest variance narrative are consistently getting better outcomes than sponsors who try to “sell” the pro forma. The market is far less punitive of a tough story than it is of an unclear story.

On multifamily specifically, the most common underwriting miss right now is still timing. Teams underestimate how long it takes for concessions to burn off and for stabilized operations to truly settle. If your plan requires perfect timing, the structure needs to absorb delay risk. If your structure cannot absorb delay risk, the leverage should come down.

And on takeouts, this is where many transactions quietly improve. When borrowers line up their likely refinance lane at origination, they make better day-one decisions on capex pacing, lease strategy, and reserve usage. When they do not, they often optimize for the first loan and get trapped at refinance.

So the practical advice this morning is simple: underwrite like you are already in year three of the loan, not month three. If the asset and structure still work under that lens, your odds of execution and refinance success go up materially.

Now a quick markets snapshot before we close.

SOFR most recently printed at three point six five.

Treasury curve at the April 2 close was about three point seven nine on the two-year, three point nine four on the five-year, four point three one on the ten-year, and four point eight eight on the thirty-year.

CMBS delinquency, per Trepp’s late-March read, is seven point five five percent.

CRE CLO issuance pace has improved meaningfully year over year, showing risk appetite has returned selectively.

And Columbus continues to show economic and housing momentum signals, even as policy and affordability pressures remain active local variables.

One thing to watch by tomorrow morning:

Watch whether front-end Treasury and SOFR stability holds while credit spreads stay contained. If rates stay orderly, transaction momentum can build quietly. If we get another volatility shock, committees will slow, leverage will compress, and execution windows will narrow again.

Final takeaway for this Thursday: discipline is now the market’s competitive advantage. The teams that are closing are not necessarily the teams with the most aggressive terms. They are the teams with the clearest operating plan, the cleanest data room, and the most realistic refinancing path. That is true for sponsors, originators, and asset managers alike.

If you are heading into lender conversations today, lead with credibility, not just upside. Show the downside plan, show the reserve logic, and show exactly how this deal performs if lease-up takes longer than hoped. In this market, clarity gets funded.

That is your Debt Desk for Thursday, April 2nd, 2026.

I will see you tomorrow.
